content.about_text: Documents

📁 Factsheet and documents

The University of Glasgow and our inspiring people have been changing the world for more than 570 years

Adam Smith laid the foundations of modern economics. James Watt gave power to the industrial revolution. Ian Donald showed us the first ultrasound image of a foetus. Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered radio pulsars.

Today, our inspiring people continue to change the world for the better. Our six crossdisciplinary areas of world-class research are shining examples of what we do best at Glasgow, each one combining a drive for pushing the boundaries of knowledge with a commitment to making a positive impact on the world:

addressing inequalities

cultural & creative economies

precision medicine & chronic diseases

one health

future life

the nano & quantum world.

content.student_tips:
#예쁜캠퍼스#수업건물이동시간 #비가많이옴

“9월-11월은 거의 모든 날 비가 왔다고 보면 됩니다. 심지어 태풍이 온 적도 있습니다. 워낙 바람을 동반한 비가 와서 접이식 우산들은 펴자마자 뒤집어져서 사실 무용지물이라고 할 수 있습니다. 다들 바람막이를 입거나 후디를 입어 머리만 안젖도록 합니다.” 
출처: “과거와 현재가 공존하는 글라스고 대학교 (2018-2019)”

“재학생인 플랫메이트도 “1년 중 비가 아예 내리지 않는 날은 5일 밖에 되지 않는다는 말도 있어”라고 말해주더라고요. 실제로 그런 것은 아니었지만 일반적으로 생각하는 영국 날씨처럼 비가 자주 오긴 합니다. 보슬비에 바람 조합이 제일 흔한데, 그 때문인지 현지인들은 우산을 쓰기 보다는 모자 달린 바람막이를 입고 다니면서 비가 오면 모자를 씁니다.”
출처: “UofG 파견보고서 (2022 봄학기)”

#박물관#식료품#산책

“학교 주변 Byres Road엔 필요한 모든 상점들이 있습니다. 옷가게는 많이 없는 편이지만 음식점이 많고 카페, 식료품점이 많습니다. Iceland shop이 Tesco나 Sainsbury's에 비하면 많이 싸니 돈을 아끼고 싶으시다면 거기서 식료품 구입하는 것을 추천드립니다.”
출처: “과거와 현재가 공존하는 글라스고 대학교 (2018-2019)”

“학교 바로 앞에 Kelvingrove Park가 있는데, 공원이 잘 조성되어 있고 언덕을 올라가면 학교 전경도 볼 수 있어서 낮에 가기에 좋아요. 글래스고에는 정말 개들이 많고 다양해서 멍멍이천국을 경험하실 수도 있습니다.” 
출처: “UofG 파견보고서 (2022 봄학기)”

#플랫형태의기숙사#아시안마트#비싼외식물가

“꼭 처음 만나서 청소 분담을 하시길 바랍니다. 저희는 화장실, 샤워실, 주방 및 복도 청소기 돌리기, 쓰레기 버리기 총 네가지로 나눠서 제비뽑기로 당번을 정하고 일주일 간격으로 돌아가면서 청소를 했고 전체가 한 번씩 업무를 끝내면 새로 제기뽑기를 하는 식으로 진행하였습니다.” 
출처: “과거와 현재가 공존하는 글라스고 대학교 (2018-2019)”

“기숙사가 제공되고, 한국인 교환학생은 보통 Cairncross나 Kelvinhaugh Street 중 하나에 배정됩니다.” 
출처: “UofG 파견보고서 (2022 봄학기)”

#경제학#예술수업#도서관뷰

“이 밖에도 백파이프 수업도 있는데(교환학생 수강료 600파운드), 스코틀랜드에서만 경험할 수 있는 특별한 기회라고 생각했습니다. 수업 편람을 잘 확인하시고 전공 밖의 다양한 수업도 알아보는 것을 추천 드려요.”
출처: “UofG 파견보고서 (2022 봄학기)”

content.accommodation:
Our International Student Residence offers accommodation for 175 people in 4 connected houses. The ISR is located directly by the river Inn. The University of Applied Sciences can be reached with a 10-minute walk along the promenade.

Applications for the upcoming academic year can be made from mid-January. Since places are in great demand, we recommend that you register as soon as possible.

For the winter semester 2024/25, all accommodation is fully booked. You are still welcome to register via the registration button, in which case you will automatically be placed on our waiting list for the winter semester 24/25.

For the summer semester 2025 registrations are possible from 05.11.2024, 10 am via the registration link on our website.

The notifications about availability and the contract transmission will be done after confirmed admission at the FH Kufstein Tirol.

Thank you for your understanding that room allocation is on a "first come - first serve" basis.

Facilities

>> 175 beds available in the International Student Residence
>> Common area with TV sets and cooking facilities

>> Sun terrace for relaxing

>> Underground parking areas for bicycles, motorcycles & cars
>> Washing machines and dryers for communal use

>> 6 workstations/computers & printers

content.about_text:
📁 Factsheet and documents

UPNA is a public university founded in 1987 by the Parliament of Navarre, with campuses in Pamplona and Tudela.

It develops its academic activity in all the areas of knowledge: Engineering, Sciences, Health Sciences, Social and Legal Sciences and Arts and Humanities.

UPNA includes an offer of 27 Bachelor's degrees (and 8 double Bachelor's degrees)  and 33 Master’s degrees (and 5 double master’s degrees), apart from a wide range of doctorate programmes and UPNA’s own degrees.

To find out more, please visit the “About UPNA” page.

content.accommodation:
기숙사는 로모노소프 모스크바 주립대학교 총장의 명령에 따라 법학부에 등록한 모든 비거주 학생에게 기숙사 숙소가 제공됩니다. 모스크바 지역에 영구적으로 거주하는 학생은 러시아 철도의 허용된 분류에 따라 거주지가 6구역보다 가깝지 않은 경우 기숙사를 제공받습니다. 경우에 따라 법학부의 요청에 따라 기숙사에 배정될 수 있는 장애인 및 기타 특권이 있는 학생의 경우 예외가 적용될 수 있습니다.

예산으로 공부하는 학생은 월 기본 장학금의 5% 이하를 지급하고 기숙사에 수용됩니다. 계약제로 입학한 학생은 해당 계약 체결 후 기숙사 배정 순서에 따라 기숙사에 배정됩니다.

계약에 따라 기숙사 입실이 제공되는 경우, 한편으로는 본교 기숙사 부서와 학생 간에 계약이 체결되고, 다른 한편으로는 당사자의 권리와 의무 및 그 준수에 대한 책임이 명시된 계약이 체결됩니다.

본교 법학부에 배정된 기숙사는 다음과 같습니다:

야세네보의 학생의 집 (DSYA), 여기에는 예산 교육 형태의 학생들이 수용됩니다

크라브첸코 거리의 학생의 집 (DSC)-예산 형태의 학습을하는 학생

베르나드스코고 거리의 학생의 집 (DSV) - 예산 교육 형태의 학생

학생 하우스 (FDS)의 지점 - 계약 형태의 교육 형태의 학생

MSU 본관 학생의 집(MSU GS) - 대학원생 및 장애 학생.

Spring 2025

We are glad to inform you that the online nomination for Spring 2025 is open now!

Academic Schedule:

Spring Semester: February 10 – June 30

Fall Semester: September 1 – January 24

Application Procedure:

All the application documents must submitted directly through the International Office of the home university to the IAAS International Department by e-mail (inter@iaas.msu.ru).

Application Deadline:

December 5, 2024 for Spring Semester 2025. We are ready to extend the deadline for several days, if there are some technical issues.

Required documents:

Certificate of nomination singed by the head of the International Office – a pdf file

Exchange student application – a pdf file and a word document

Statement of purpose – a pdf file and a word document

Academic reference (transcript) – a pdf file

Passport scan (the first two-page opening) – a pdf file

Health Certificate (negative Aids and Tuberculosis tests results) – bring to Moscow

International Health Insurance Certificate – bring to Moscow

6 passport size photos (3x4 cm on mat paper) – bring to Moscow

Please, note that the applicant's passport should be valid for at least 18 months after the beginning of the planned study period!
content.student_tips:
#매우크다#러시아의겨울#높은본관

봄 학기이지만 2월부터 6월까지 날씨는 춥습니다. 눈과 바람이 있기 때문에 따뜻한 옷이 필수입니다. 5월까지는 많이 춥지만 6월부터는 조금씩 풀리기 시작하고 따뜻해집니다. 러시아의 여름은 해 뜨면 습도도 없고 하늘도 광활하고 예뻐서 구경하기 좋습니다.
출처: “모스크바에서의 한 학기 (2022 봄학기)”

러시아는 여름 하루가 굉장히 긴 나라입니다. 해가 일찍 떠서 늦게 집니다. 날씨는 한국의 날씨와 비슷합니다. 하지만 우기가 내립니다. 우기만 제외하면 대체로 맑은 날씨를 볼 수 있습니다. 많은 사람들이 러시아라면 많이 겨울이 많이 춥다고 하지만 생각보다 춥지 않았습니다. 기온은 한국과 비슷하거나 오히려 더 따뜻했습니다. 다만 한국에서 자주 볼 수 없는 눈은 정말 많이 옵니다. 아주 짧은 시간에 눈이 엄청 쌓이기도 합니다.
출처: “모스크바 국립 대학에서의 한학기 (2019년 봄학기)”

모스크바 가을 날씨가 매우 좋습니다. 가을엔 따뜻하며 선선한 날씨이기 때문에 놀러 다니기 딱 좋습니다. 하지만 9월부터 추워져서 감기 조심해야 합니다. 나가기 전에 날씨 확인하는 것은 필수입니다. 겨울에는 햇빛이 거의 없고 눈도 많이 내립니다. 따뜻하게 입고 다닌다면 생각보다 춥지 않게 느껴집니다. 모스크바는 모자, 장갑 그리고 따뜻한 양말이 필수품입니다. 눈이 많이 내려서 길이 꽁꽁 얼고 미끄러워지고 또 눈이 녹으면서 길이 더러워집니다. 아끼는 신발을 버릴 수도 있습니다.

출처: “모스크바 엠게우 교환학생 경험보고서(2018-2019년 가을학기)”

#까삐똘리#사진#스베르은행

#경제학과식당#스탈로바야#빨래방

#소규모수업#도서관출입증#세미나신청

경제학과 복수 전공하는 학생은 경제학과 수업도 들을 수 있습니다. 교환학생이 듣는 영어 수업을 듣다가 이싸에서 경제학과 수업을 들을 수 있게 허가를 받아서 써 달라고 얘기해주면 됩니다. 그리고 경제학과 담당자로부터 수업에 관한 정보를 전달 받을 수 있습니다. 또한 공부할 수 있는 열람실은 게제의читательный зал으로 가야 합니다. 그리고 도서관 책은 방으로 가져갈 수 없습니다.
출처: “ 교환학생 파견보고서 (2018년 여름학기)”

content.about_text:
Courses and academic structure

Full time studies for one semester is 30 credits (ECTS).

Students must fulfill the specific prerequisites for each course they apply for.

Students are admitted to courses on a first come, first served basis, based on their application date. The possibilities to switch courses after the application is processed are limited and there is no add/drop period after arrival to Linnaeus University.

Each semester consists of four consecutive periods (not parallel) and courses can last for one or several periods. A full time (100%) course gives a full study load for the period(s) the course lasts, while part time courses (25-75% study load) should be combined with other course(s) in order to reach a full study load in total for each period.

List of courses open for exchange students

In the course list you can filter for faculty, semester, campus, field of studies etc. By clicking on a course you will get more information about the content of the course and its prerequisites.

The Beginners’ Swedish Language courses may be selected as a part of the regular study load, or as extra courses.

Further information

The semester dates can be found in the academic calendar. Mandatory Orientation for new students is arranged a couple of days before the start of the semester.

Useful information about studying at Linnaeus University as an exchange student may also be found on our website.

Homepage: Exchange studies | lnu.se

Youtube: http://www.youtube.com/user/linneuni

Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/linnaeusuniversity

Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/linnaeusuniversity/?hl=eng

Linnaeus University’s Erasmus film: Erasmus 30 years - LnuPlay - Linnaeus University MediaSpace

•Students can take courses on 3rd year undergraduate and graduate study (must be graduate students).

Applicants must have completed 2 years of undergraduate study at the start of the exchange program.
Students must be graduate students to take graduate courses.

"First cycle" in Chalmers is year 1-3 at the bachelor's level. "Second cycle" is year 4-5 at the master's level. Master's introduction courses are also included in the first cycle.

Swedish language classes are not offered at Chalmers, but students can turn to Folkuniversitetet, which is a private college in the city centre of Gothenburg where students can take Swedish classes on different levels with a student discount. http://www.folkuniversitetet.se/In-English/Swedish-courses/swedish_goteborg/

📔 Module(Course) Catalogue

Courses

We are pleased to inform you that the Ca' Foscari School for International Education (SIE) will once again be offering for-credit Italian Language courses and English-taught courses to incoming students in mobility at no cost during the second semester of the 2024/2025 academic year.

English-taught courses

[SIE001] Contemporary History of Venice

[SIE032] History of Italian Culture and Literature – Modern and Contemporary

[SIE066] Venetian Art and Archaeology – 2: Venetian Heritage in the Adriatic and in the Mediterranean

[SIE080] The Law of the New Space Economy

[SIE090] Introduction to Latin for International Students

Each course provides 6 CFU/ECTS.

Attendance is generally not mandatory, but teachers may ask all students to attend a minimum number of lessons and/or assign some additional bibliography to non-attending students.

Italian Language courses

[SIE105A1] Italian language for Exchange students - Module A: Communication and culture - Beginner A1

[SIE106A1] Italian language for Exchange students - Module B: Forms, structures, and meanings - Beginner A1

[SIE105A2] Italian language for Exchange students - Module A: Communication and culture - Elementary A2

[SIE106A2] Italian language for Exchange students - Module B: Forms, structures, and meanings - Elementary A2

[SIE107] Italian language for Exchange Students - Pre-intermediate B1

[SIE108] Italian language for Exchange Students - Intermediate B2

Each course provides 6 CFU/ECTS and it combines a General Language module with Language Practice classes.

Attendance is mandatory: students must attend at least 80% of classes for each module.

All students must take an online Placement Test

Schedule and syllabi

The overall schedule has already been made available on the website. The complete syllabi of all courses will be published approximately in early February.

How to register

Students can register for all courses by filling out the online Application Form which they received via email.

Each course will only be activated if it reaches a minimum number of participants.

Registration will be limited to a maximum number of students per class. Priority will be given in order of having filled out this form. Applicants not added to the class will be placed on a waitlist, and will be added to the course if a place becomes available.

How to take the Placement Test

Students can take the online Placement Test at the link which they received via email. They must log in to the Moodle platform either with their matricola (student enrolment number) and password or by using their provisional student account.

Deadline

The deadline for the Application Form (all courses) and for the Placement Test (Italian language courses) is January 13, 2025.

Guidelines

Italian language courses: www.unive.it/italian-exchange

English-taught courses: www.unive.it/courses-exchange

Syllabi and schedules

The complete syllabi and the schedules of all courses will be available on the website approximately in early September.

Students can register for all courses by filling out the online Application Form which they received via email.

Each course will only be activated if it reaches a minimum number of participants.

Registration will be limited to a maximum number of students per class. Priority will be given in order of having filled out this form. Applicants not added to the class will be placed on a waitlist, and will be added to the course if a place becomes available.

To take the online Placement Test, students need to already have their matricola (student enrolment number) and password. They can take the test at the link which they received via email.

The deadline for the Application Form (all courses) is August 6, 2024.

The deadline for the Placement Test (Italian language courses) is August 20, 2024.

content.about_text:
We would like to anticipate that the Bocconi Christmas break will run from December 21, 2024, to January 6, 2025, included.

During this period the International Student Desk office will not be available. We invite students to take these dates into consideration for any assistance they might need.

Should your Spring 25 students need assistance with their visa application process, we invite you to encourage them to contact the ISD office at inexchange@unibocconi.itas soon as possible, to receive guidance in due time before the Christmas break.

Please find on the ISD website the steps that your students need to follow for their visa application.

Please note that students in need of the most recent version of the official Bocconi Acceptance Letter can download it directly from the Admissions portal (the platform where students completed the Exchange Program online application), by accessing it with their Bocconi credentials, even if the portal is closed.

Some visa application procedures may vary from one consulate to the other. Therefore, students should follow the visa application instructions provided on the website of the consulate where they are going to apply. Please also note that it may take longer to schedule a visa appointment and there may be some delays in obtaining a visa, so it's important that your students complete the process well in advance.

✏️ Academic Requirements

📔 Module(Course) Catalogue

Study Plan Guidelines

Undergraduate students can select courses at the undergraduate level only (courses with codes 30xxx), while graduate students can select both undergraduate and graduate classes (courses with codes 20xxx and some 50xxx).

Some courses may have specific restrictions: carefully check the "Suggested background knowledge" section available in the course profile.

Course offer for 2024/25

Dear exchange student,

the 2024-2025 Educational Offer for exchange students is now available at http://www.ir.unibocconi.eu/coe

Courses that are not included in the Fall 24 course list are not open to Fall 24 exchange students and will not appear on the course selection platform.

The Fall 24 online course selection will run from August 22 at 3pm (Italian time) to August 27 at 3pm (Italian time). You will have the chance to modify your study plan during the Add/Drop period from September 10 to September 17. After September 17 no changes to the study plan will be allowed.

We will e-mail you a step-by-step guide about the course selection by the end of July. For the time being, we invite you to read the study plan guidelines below.

Study Plan Guidelines

Undergraduate students can select courses at the undergraduate level only (courses with codes 30xxx), while graduate students can select both undergraduate and graduate classes (courses with codes 20xxx).

You are required to select from a minimum of 1 up to a maximum of 5 courses per semester.

Courses are assigned on a first-come-first served basis, therefore we recommend that you expand your tentative course list, in order to have a higher chance of being allocated to your preferred Bocconi classes (three Academic Advisors are available to advise you on academic contents only).

Some courses may have specific restrictions: carefully check the "Suggested background knowledge" section available in the course profile.

Students who wish to take all the exams by the December exam session (December 9-21, 2024), have to make sure that the classes they select have no exam time conflicts. The exam schedule will be published at www.unibocconi.eu/exams by early August.

Incoming exchange students can sit their exams in the sessions that best fit their needs, provided they are scheduled within their exchange period (Academic Year 2024-2025): December, January and late January/early February. If two final exams are scheduled on the same day and at the same time, students can select both courses and sit the exams in separate exam sessions, if available.

We recommend that you check the most important 2024-2025 Academic Year dates - including the Earliest Departure Date (December 22, 2024) - at www.ir.unibocconi.eu/calendar.

Please note: all exams must be sat in presence, on official dates and on Bocconi premises only (the online option is not allowed).

The class timetable is available at didattica.unibocconi.eu/lezioni

Best regards,

ISD Team

universities.restricted_areas:
Pharmacy, Nursing, Education*, Computer Science, Computer Engineering, Accounting, and students needing a full courseload in Psychology are not able to take coursework at USF.  Biology, Finance, Math, Chemistry, Supply Chain, Economics, and all Engineering majors have strict prerequisites and additional review to confirm course requirements are met prior to course approval. Advising staff in these areas to review your transcripts and course syllabi prior to issuing registration permissions and approval is not guaranteed.  

Home institution course approval does not guarantee course approval at USF as the University has a strict state mandated pre-requisite format for coursework.  Transcripts must be evaluated to confirm requirements are met for requested classes. 
Exchange students may need additional permissions to register even if they do not see a prerequisite. Coursework is also restricted by student standing, for example, it may be open to only upper classmen or Junior/Senior standing – this means it is open to third- and fourth-year students. Registration permissions will need to be obtained from the department for these classes. 
Exchange students attending USF for two semesters submit course requests both semesters to the inbound exchange coordinator so they can facilitate course approval as appropriate. 
 
Exchange students may not take Internship and/or thesis classes in their first semester of exchange
 
Students who are wanting to take an elective in the College of the Arts, must wait until every end of the semester to register as classes are restricted until late December for Spring and mid to late August for Fall. 

* While the majority of courses in the College of Education are unavailable, there are a select few offered each semester that are available but not guaranteed. Incoming exchange students may not register in practicum, classroom placement, or other courses tied to education licensure.  Please contact the incoming exchange advisor for more information.

The residential homes are not just for Erfurt students, but also encompasses other college towns such as Jena, Weimar, Ilmenau, Nordhausen, and Schmalkalden.

Rent is paid in an all-inclusive manner, including utilities such as water, heat, and light.

Minimal rental period is one semester.

When rooms are furnished, including a bed, table, chair, wardrobe and shelves. Some rooms have modern kitchens, where you can use cookers or refrigerators, but kitchen equipment is not included. However, it is important to note that some rooms may not be furnished.

There are a number of options for renting, including shared apartments, single apartments, or shared apartments with their own bathroom. Due to the fact there are filters for location and rent pricing, there are a number of options for each student.

content.about_text:
Application process for nominated students

What happens after the nomination?

International Mobility Services will create usernames to our online application system SoleMOVE. Usernames and application instructions are then sent directly to the students by e-mail after which they can log in to the system and fill in the online application.

APPLICATION

Nominated students are able to submit an application based on active/renewed bilateral agreements.

Application deadlines:

Applications for the autumn semester (Sep-Dec): 30 April

Applications for the academic year (Sep-May): 30 April

Applications for the spring semester (Jan-May): 30 September

Applications for traineeships: above mentioned deadlines recommended

Please note that it is not possible to apply after the deadlines.  Applications arriving after 30 April will be considered only for the spring semester 2025. Applications arriving after 30 September will be rejected.

Information on application procedure:

For exchange students

Information on courses for exchange students:

Information on courses is available in Peppi. Students should choose courses from one campus only and the choice of courses defines the campus they will be studying on. Also, majority of courses (not ECTS) should be from one campus and from department/school/subject. Nominated students receive more detailed instructions.

Acceptance:

The faculties of the University of Eastern Finland admit students. After receiving the application,  International Mobility Services will forward it to the appropriate academic department. The department then processes the application and the proposed learning agreement.

Once International Mobility Services receive an official confirmation of acceptance from the faculty/department, an official admission document is issued and sent to the student by e-mail 4-6 weeks after the application period has ended. At the same time, s/he will receive information on practical matters.

content.accommodation:
Student Housing Company Joensuun Elli

Merimiehenkatu 30

tel. +358 (0)13 337 7800

e-mail: kodit@joensuunelli.fi

Check the website for opening hours

Accommodation in Joensuu for both international and Finnish students is offered by Joensuun Elli (opens in a new tab), an independent Student Housing Company that provides housing for more than 3000 students. Joensuun Elli has several housing units located in different parts of Joensuu and  they are situated within 0.5–7 km from the Joensuu campus. Most students live in a shared flat where they have a private room and they share kitchen, bathroom, and toilet with 1-4 other students. Exchange students are usually accommodated in a furnished room with a bed and a mattress, a desk, a chair and a wardrobe. There are no curtains, rugs, linen, dishes, cutlery, etc. in the flats. The Student Union rents survival packages to cover the basic needs. Both Finnish and international degree and post-graduate students are normally accommodated in a room without furniture (only wardrobe). Students arriving with their family (i.e. spouse and/or children) may apply for a two-bedroom family flat. Please note that you can apply a family apartment also with a friend or a relative.

Rent for a room in a shared flat is about 190-300 euros/month. Rent includes electricity, heating and water. Tenants who have a furnished room need to pay a reasonable additional fee for the furniture. In all flats there is a possibility to have the ElliNet internet connection for 20 euros/month. There are no computers in the flats, and in order to be able to use the internet connection, tenants may have to buy a suitable adapter for their personal computer. Note that the internet will not be available right away. (There are also other possibilities, for example, a mobile broadband modem that you can purchase from a tele-communications company and is usually a cheaper alternative.). There is a free laundry room and a public sauna in every housing unit. Private sauna or a parking space for car can be reserved for an extra fee. There are no meals or cleaning service available.

How to apply for housing (opens in a new tab) with Joensuun Elli. Fill out the application carefully. Please note that if your stay requires a Resident Permit, you should apply for housing only after you have received the permit.

As soon as you receive an accommodation offer by email, you should reply whether you accept or refuse the offered accommodation. Usually you will receive a housing offer about one month before your planned arrival. Acceptance of offered accommodation is legally binding and will be considered as a signature on the lease. Rent must be paid from the confirmed date onwards. Unconfirmed reservations will automatically be cancelled and the applicant will lose the accommodation offered and the application will no longer be valid.

A tenant’s lease begins always on the first day of the month and it can be made for full months only, ending on the last day of the month. This means that the tenant has to pay the rent for a whole month even if he or she does not need the apartment for the entire period.

For further information about apartments, their location, applying, etc., see the Joensuun Elli website (opens in a new tab).

content.about_text:
York is an internationally recognised university with a reputation for quality, situated on a beautiful parkland campus close to  one of Britain’s most historic cities, one of the best cities in the UK to live and visit. We place an emphasis on small class sizes,  personal attention and guidance while league tables regularly place us as one of the top UK universities.

∙ Full integration in regular degree programme modules

∙ A personal/academic supervisor to support you and your studies

∙ An academic transcript at the end of your studies

∙ A lively and stimulating environment

∙ Academic study in one or more departments (timetable allowing)

∙ Study periods of one, two or three terms of the academic year

∙ Academic credit for modules to the equivalent of 40-60 credits per term

∙ College membership and access to a wide range of extra-curricular activities

Our campus

The University of York has a lively, cosmopolitan campus with its own shops, banks, bars and restaurants, making it an  exciting place to live and work, with everything you need close at hand. Our campus is compact, easy to get around and close  to the city centre and has a safe, friendly atmosphere.

The campus occupies three sites: Campus West, Campus East which are characterised by large lakes, landscaped parkland  and lots of student activity, and Kings Manor - one of historic York's most attractive and unusual sites.

Our ongoing investment in new colleges, teaching and learning space, laboratories, research facilities and a new sports village  means it has never been a better time to join the University of York!

Transcript

Undergraduate official marks are due to be released for students on 8th July, with official transcripts being produced later in July. We do not have an official date yet for when these will be produced and sent out. Taught postgraduate marks are externally moderated in the October following an academic year of teaching and so we send transcripts with taught postgraduate marks in the November / December following a student’s exchange.

Students who were enrolled in both undergraduate and postgraduate modules will receive one official transcript for their undergraduate modules at the end of July and a further official transcript containing their postgraduate marks in November/December.

Digital copies of official transcripts will be emailed to students on their personal email address and to their home institution. We unfortunately do not send physical copies of official transcripts anymore.

* Study Abroad and International Exchange students can usually (if the agreement with your home university allows) choose modules from across our departments (excluding the College of Medicine and Health, Liberal Arts, and the Graduate School of Education) and at any undergraduate level. However, please note the following:
- You must meet any pre-requisites stated in the module description
- You must choose a level suitable for your prior knowledge of the subject area
- Choosing modules from across different departments and levels will increase the possibility of timetable clashes. This can make finalising your study programme more difficult. Therefore you should choose a minimum of 50% of your modules in one subject area.
- We are unable to guarantee that the modules you initially choose will be available. Your study programme will be finalised in the first two weeks of your arrival at the University of Exeter.

content.about_text:
Exeter is ranked 153rd in the prestigious QS World University Rankings 2024 and 11th in the UK according to The Times and The Sunday Times University Guide 2024.

Exeter is a member of the Russell Group of research-intensive universities and at the forefront of global collaboration with over 300+ partnerships worldwide.

Exeter has been rated Gold in the Teaching Excellence Framework (TEF) 2023 – a gold rating recognises a university for delivering consistently outstanding teaching.

We can trace our roots back to 1855 and continue to deliver world-class teaching and research from exceptional locations in the South West of England.

More than 6000 international students from 130 different countries contribute to our friendly, inclusive and supportive community.

We’re consistently ranked in the top 10 in the British Universities and Colleges Sport (BUCS) rankings.

The University of Exeter is ranked 13th on a global list of experts in “climatic processes” and our Green Futures campaign will drive action on the environment and climate emergency.

Orientation programmes at the start of the semester help you to get to know the campus, and with over 300 societies and 50 sport clubs to join there are plenty of opportunities to get involved in university life.

Study Abroad at the University of Exeter

Instagram

Tiktok
content.campus_location:
Link to Google drive folder with images

The following modules are popular for exchange students:

British Cultures: The Exeter Seminar (15 and 30 credit versions)

This module is designed for inbound exchange/study abroad students and provides a critical introduction to some familiar, and not so familiar, aspects of British Culture from the early modern period through to the present day. The module covers a variety of cultural forms (including literature and film) and genres (including drama, fiction and poetry) and encourages students to reflect on the processes by which – sometimes competing – ideas about national identity have been created, disseminated and interpreted. Teaching is by regular seminars and occasional workshops, some of which will draw on the resources of the University’s Special Collections and Bill Douglas Cinema Museum. Assessment is by a reflective learning log, a group presentation, and an independent study.

The Foreign Language Centre

The Language Centre at the University of Exeter is a great place to learn a language. As well as first-class learning resources, they provide excellent teaching by suitably qualified and experienced professionals.

The languages they offer include British Sign Language, Cornish, French, German, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Mandarin Chinese, Russian, Spanish and Ukrainian.  All the FLC Undergraduate modules count for credits.  Although the modules are full year, the Centre will allow inbound, semester one students to study the first half of a module for 15 credits, something the Modern Languages department does not permit.  If your students would like to continue with a language they are already studying, or learn a new language, they can do so at the Centre.

Modules in Academic and Professional English  (for non-native English speakers)

The English language modules offered by the English Language Skills Development team are an excellent option for inbound students. These modules are 15 Exeter credits (7.5 ECT) and RQF5. They are suitable for students with an IELTS level 6.5 to 8 (CEFR B2-C1). In addition to offering students an opportunity to further develop their English language, cultural competence and practical skills that can be applied to other modules, the emphasis on criticality and reflective approaches gives these modules the academic rigour expected of any undergraduate module. They also provide transferable skills for beyond the Exeter experience and on to further study and future employability.

·         ELC2727 Academic Listening and Speaking – developing skills for giving critical presentations and for effective participation in academic discussions.

·         ELC2728 – Academic Reading and Writing – emphasising critical approaches and the development of argument.

·         ELC2729 – International English: Contexts and Varieties – expanding intercultural awareness, language knowledge and critical academic skills.

·         ELC2730 – Professional English – developing workplace English whilst also critically reflecting on the challenges of using English as a global language for work.

If students have already submitted their study plans, they will be able to review their choices during the first two weeks of the semester, the ‘add/drop’ period.  We hold module drop-in sessions where we assist students with any module queries and/or changes.

there are no new restrictions at this time. However, as a reminder, here are our current restrictions:

Our study abroad programme is designed for undergraduate students. Most students will only be able to take undergraduate units (Years 1–3). A few schools offer M-level units, but Master’s students should only be nominated if they are able to take UG units as part of their study abroad programme.

Psychological Science:
This subject is taught as an experimental science at Bristol, not as a social science. Students should have a strong background in Chemistry, Biology, and Neuroscience. The school recommends students take no more than one third-year unit per semester, and second-year unit availability is limited.

Engineering:
Unit availability varies by programme, and single-semester students should be flexible with their choices. 

Mechanical Engineering: Limited 1st and 2nd year units.

Civil Engineering: Few units available for single semester students. Usually full-year study is recommended.

Aerospace Engineering: Limited lower-year units.

Electrical & Electronic Engineering: Very few units across all years, particularly for single semester students.

Our updated factsheet for 2026/27 will be shared with partners in January 2026.

content.about_text:
We are happy to announce that we will be launching three Study Themes at Bristol from
2024/25, available to General Pathway Study Abroad students. Each Study Theme brings
together units from across different departments and schools that share a common theme,
enabling students to encounter the topic from a range of different disciplines and
approaches. We hope these themes will help students identify units under a common topic
and enhance their academic experience whilst at Bristol.

In addition to being interdisciplinary, Study Themes are also inspired by the city of Bristol
and its rich history and contemporary life. They are:

Global Citizen: the British Experience – with units on literature, history, theatre,
visual culture, film, policy studies, archaeology and more.

The World We Live In: Environment & Sustainability – with units on
environmental humanities, geography, earth science, policy studies, history, literature,
cultural studies and more.

Social Justice and Activism – with units on policy studies, education, sociology,
history, literature, black humanities, politics and more.

Study Themes are intended as a tool to help students navigate the wide range of unit
choices at Bristol by making our offerings on these popular topics more visible. Students
can pick all their units from a single Study Theme, or just one—there are no rules! There is
no need to apply to a specific Study Theme. General Pathway students are simply invited
to browse the selection and see what interests them!

For more details as to specific units, check out the Study Guides for these new themes,
available on our website in February.

It is a requirement for all study abroad students to complete the assessments for all of the units they are registered on at Bristol. Spaces on units are highly competitive and a lot of effort goes into negotiating places on those units for our inbound students, therefore it is quite problematic if a student who is placed on those units decides not to complete the assessment which may result in failure of the unit as that place could have gone to another student who needed the class to meet their academic requirements.

content.campus_location:
Nottingham is located in the heart of the Midlands and has excellent road and rail links to major cities across the UK.

There are easy transport links to the local East Midlands Airport as well as major airports in Birmingham, Manchester and London – making it accessible from anywhere in the world.

Being in the middle of the country means students don’t have far to go to get to all the top sights of the UK. Nottingham is significantly cheaper than London but is only a 1 and half to 2 hour train ride away. We have access to a regional airport with flights to 27 different countries, which is a short bus ride away or an International Airport within an hour drive or an hour and half by train.

Nottingham is a medium sized city, it’s compact so you can easily walk across the city centre but there’s a lot in a small space. It has a very vibrant café and restaurant culture, lots of independent shopping as well as the big brands. A huge range of bars and live music venues.

The campuses are well connected to the city centre, with many bus routes and tram into the city, taking about 15mins.

We have two football stadiums, a cricket ground which hosts International Test Matches, a National Ice Centre and a national Water Sports Centre.

content.about_text:
Timeline

We send a UoE transcript by email to each visiting student’s home institution.

Semester 1 students' transcripts are sent in February

Full Year and Semester 2 students' transcripts are sent in August

Transcript contact email address

Emails are sent to the transcript contact email address students provide in their application, so please make sure your students know the email address they should use. This will avoid delays in receiving transcripts.

Students can use their MyEd portal to:

Request additional copies of their official transcript via the Electronic Document Service

Arrange for transcripts to be automatically sent to a third party

Transcripts include a key to the Edinburgh grading system and recommended equivalencies. Receiving institutions decide how they interpret UoE grades and transfer credit.

Postgraduate modules are available to postgraduate study abroad/exchange students only.
Modules with examinations are not open to Semester 1-only Study Abroad/Exchange students.

universities.admission_notes:
We have no requirement on what major/department students have to study in Yonsei for admissions to exchange at SOAS.

Undergraduate applicants should have the equivalent of a British 2:1 in their current undergraduate studies. For example, North American students should have a GPA of 3.3/4.0 and students from Norway should be receiving predominantly grades B and C. Applicants with a GPA of 3.0, or equivalent, will be considered if they can demonstrate a strong academic motivation or have a higher GPA in their major.

📁 Factsheet and documents

SOAS University of London is the leading Higher Education institution in Europe specialising in the study of Asia, Africa and the Near and Middle East.

SOAS is a remarkable institution. With our vast repository of knowledge and expertise on our specialist regions, we are uniquely placed to inform and shapecurrent thinking about the economic, political, cultural, security and religious challenges of our world. Our programmes are taught by respected academics engaged in fieldwork and research which influences government policy and the lives of individuals acrossthe globe. SOAS scholars grapple with the pressing issues confronting two-thirds of humankind today: democracy, development, economy, finance, public and corporate policy, human rights, migration, identity, legalsystems, poverty, religion, and social change

content.about_text:
Please do ensure that your students are also prompt in applying and have all the necessary paperwork (transcripts, English language certificates etc) ready to submit.  Students will be able to apply for the programme between the 1st September and 31st October, and no late applications will be accepted (partners who also send fee-paying students, please see our dates and deadlines page for fee-paying application periods as these differ to exchange students).  A reminder of all our dates and deadlines can be found on our webpage.

Research students do not need to be nominated via the online form, please email studyabroad@kcl.ac.uk with their details and we will advise further.

MODULE INFORMATION AND DEPARTMENTS OR MODULES WITH RESTRICTIONS

Students nominated under a specific departmental agreement will be required to take at least half of their modules from the nominated department regardless of their major.   Students nominated under a college-wide agreement (including Circle U) do not have this requirement.  We cannot guarantee registration on any specific module or from a specific department, so students should be flexible on what they request especially if they are planning on taking level 4 modules outside of their major.  Students must pay close attention to our eligibility criteria when considering their module options, and should be aware that level 4 modules fill up very quickly.  Please avoid nominating students whose major is not taught at King’s, or is very different to the department they are being nominated under, to avoid disappoint when selecting modules.

Please read the information on our restricted departments, which are listed at the bottom of our Guidance for Overseas Partners & Advisors page as well as on our module selection page as there are changes from last year.  Our main restrictions apply to English Literature, History, Film (heavily restricted, please note that we only accept Film theory as appropriate background for modules from the Film department) and Informatics (Computer Science), with additional restrictions to European and International Studies, Global Health and Social Medicine and Law.  Students should be aware of these restrictions when applying to King’s in the first instance, as well as whether their major is actually offered to avoid disappointment.

We are happy to announce that for semester 2, Informatics will be open to Computer Science majors nominated by any partner university.  Students will be registered on a maximum of two Informatics modules in the first instance, and may have the opportunity to request more in our module amendment period, subject to availability.  Please be aware that module space still remains limited and we cannot guarantee registration on any specific Informatics module.

English and History will now be open to fee-paying English and History majors in the first instance, rather than just exchange students.

We reserve the right to reinstate any restrictions next academic year.  Please note we cannot sign any Learning Agreements nor provide module outlines until students have been registered on their modules.

IMPORTANT NOTE REGARDING PROOF OF ENGLISH PROFICIENCY

Please refer to our undergraduate and postgraduate English language entry requirements under Band B.  If students do not have one of the English tests/certificates listed on the website, Admissions may request a letter from the student’s home university as proof of English proficiency if applicable.  Admissions will provide the template which must be followed exactly.

ARRIVAL IN LONDON, EXAM PERIOD AND TRANSCRIPT DATES

We expect students to be in London to participate in our compulsory Orientation week between 6th – 10th January 2025, or at least to have arrived in time for the start of teaching on Monday 13th January.  Late arrivals may be withdrawn from the programme if they have not been given explicit permission by King’s to arrive after the start of teaching.

Semester 2 students are expected to sit any in-person exams scheduled in our May exam period, and should be prepared to stay in London for the entirety of the programme until the exam timetable is released in March.  Exams or assessments will not be moved to accommodate personal travel plans.  Students can see all our programme and exam dates on the website.

Transcripts will be released via GradIntelligence in mid July.  You will be sent a notification by GradIntel and will need to log into the website to download student transcripts.  If you cannot remember your password you will need to reset it via the GradIntel website.  We cannot provide provisional transcripts nor results any earlier than mid July.  Students who need to resit exams or resubmit coursework will receive an updated transcript in September.

Students should use studyabroad@kcl.ac.uk for any queries.

📁 Factsheet and documents
content.campus_location:
Google Maps

Campus Map

Virtual Campus Tour

Study Abroad

Academic Requirements

Students should have a cumulative GPA of 3.3 or above on a 4.0 scale or its equivalent

Students must have the necessary academic background to be considered for their preferred modules

Minimum Language Requirement

If students require a Student Visa, they must meet the specific English Language requirements as specified by UK Visas and Immigration, as detailed on our website: https://www.kcl.ac.uk/abroad/applying/entry-requirements

📔 Module(Course) Catalogue

Only modules that are listed on the Study Abroad Module Catalogue are available to Study Abroad students. Not all modules at King's College London are open to Study Abroad students

Each King's module has a code attached to it, which begins with a number - 4, 5, 6 or 7

4 is a first-year undergraduate module (introductory) - modules at this level are generally open to everyone but some subjects, such as the Sciences, Music and Informatics, require relevant and sufficient background even at introductory level.

5 is a second-year undergraduate module (intermediate) - modules at this level require students to have already taken 3-4+ modules in this subject area at their home institution

6 is a final year undergraduate module (advanced) - modules at this level require students to have already taken 6-7+ modules in this subject area at their home institution

Modules with codes beginning with 7 are for postgraduate students only

Undergraduate students cannot take Level 7 modules, and Postgraduate students cannot take Levels 4, 5 or 6.

Our eligibility criteria are strict and it is your responsibility to ensure you meet the criteria before submitting your module choices. You must review our Eligibility Criteria tab and the module registration guidance before looking through the module catalogues and choosing your modules.

If the transcript you used for your King’s application did not contain the modules you were studying for at the time of application or in the current semester, we strongly advise you to send an up-to-date transcript/record of registration to studyabroad@kcl.ac.uk, even if you have yet to receive grades for them. (For this we can accept a screenshot of your courseload if your home university does not issue transcripts with in-progress modules. Make sure this screenshot clearly shows this is your record.)

If you transferred between universities during your studies, please also make sure we have all the transcripts. These must be in English.

Some modules have specific prerequisites specified under the module details page. Please make sure you have covered this material (at King’s in your first semester, or at home; you will be asked to show evidence of this if not clear from your transcript) before requesting these modules. Students need to meet any prerequisites for their desired modules in addition to meeting the wider eligibility criteria.

Places on modules are not guaranteed for any student due to class size restrictions and are allocated on a first-come, first-served basis. We do not have waiting lists.

Study Abroad Tutors are required to send your transcript to the Study Abroad Tutor in the relevant department since their approval is required before registration

Please only request Music modules if you can play an instrument and/or read music fluently. If you have no Music modules on your transcript, please inform us of your musical background and ability when you submit your Module Request Form. This is so that we can provide the academic tutor with this supporting information in order for them to sufficiently assess your eligibility.

Law modules are almost all Level 6, but can be actually taken by 2nd year students, so they fall under the Level 5 requirements (above). For Law modules open to non-Law students, an equivalent background in Social Science, Politics or Business (depending on the module's subject) would be required.

⚠️ Module Restrictions

For those students nominated under a specific departmental exchange agreement, you will be required to take at least 50% of your credits in that department. You may be nominated under a different department to your major, please check with your home university if you are unsure. We will also remind you of your nominated department closer to the opening of module registration

The table below lists which departments undergraduate students can request from depending on your nominated department.  It also indicates other departments that may offer related modules (although these will not count towards the 50% threshold of modules you must take from your nominated department).

Postgraduate students do not have the same flexibility and should request at least 50% of modules from the specific nominated department only.

If your exchange department is not listed (for example, Law or Theology and Religious Studies) then you are unlikely to find related modules outside of that department.

Students nominated under specific English (Literature) department agreements or Literature majors from National University of Singapore, University of California, UNC Chapel Hill, University of Pennsylvania, University of Hong Kong, University of British Columbia, University of Michigan, University of Sydney will be prioritised for modules within the English Department in the first instance.

Subject to availability, modules will then be offered to Literature majors from all exchange partners during our module amendment period. Fee-paying students will not be permitted to register for English Literature modules.

Postgraduate modules in English are only open to postgraduate students nominated under English or Comparative Literature specific agreements.

Level 6 modules are prioritised for students from specific exchange partners (see individual module catalogue pages for details) in the first instance.  Subject to availability, level 6 modules will then be offered to Politics/IR/Social Sciences major students during our module amendment period.

Postgraduate modules in EIS are only open to postgraduate students nominated under EIS specific agreements.

Students nominated under specific Film department agreements as well as Film/Cinema theory major students from other exchange partners will be prioritised for modules within the Film Department in the first instance.  Subject to availability, modules will then be offered to non-Film major exchange students in our module amendment period. Fee-paying students will not be permitted to register for Film modules.  Please note that we do not count practical film-making modules towards our eligibility criteria - all relevant modules must be theory based.

Exchange students from the University of Oslo, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, the University of Melbourne, National University of Singapore, Western University, Cornell University or York University, as well as fee-paying students, will be prioritised for modules within the GHSM Department.  Subject to availability, modules will then be offered to other exchange students in our module amendment period.

Students nominated under specific History department agreements as well as History major students from other exchange partners will be prioritised for modules within the History Department in the first instance.  Subject to availability, modules will then be offered to non-History major exchange students in our module amendment period.  Full year History modules are open to all full year exchange students (subject to meeting eligibility criteria).  Modules will then be offered to History major fee-paying students, subject to availability.  Please note there is no guarantee that fee-paying students will be able to register for History modules.

Computer Science majors from École Polytechnique, University of Hong Kong, University of Melbourne, Monash University, National University of Singapore, University of Toronto, University of California, UNC Chapel Hill, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, Université Catholique de Lille will be prioritised for modules within the Informatics Department but will initially be registered on a maximum of two modules from the Informatics Department.

Subject to availability, modules will then be offered to Computer Science majors from all exchange partners during our module amendment period. Fee-paying students will not be permitted to register for Informatics modules.

Postgraduate modules in Informatics are only open to postgraduate students nominated under Informatics specific agreements.

Modules marked as Law only are only open to students specifically registered on a Law programme (LLB, BA Law or equivalent, not Legal Studies). Modules marked as available to non-Law students are open to students on other social sciences degrees such as Legal Studies, as well as Law or Business, depending on the module focus.

Modules from the Psychology Department (outside of the Biosciences catalogue) are only open to full-year students, with prior approval from the Psychology Department which is granted on a case-by-case basis. Please contact studyabroad@kcl.ac.uk for more information.

Postgraduate students cannot take undergraduate modules

Students must ensure that they meet all of the prerequisites listed on the module catalogue in order to be eligible to take intermediate and advanced modules

Semester 1-only students are expected to sit in-person exams that take place in December, or any online exams that take place in our January assessment period although you are not required to physically be in London for the online exams. If you are assessed by coursework only, deadlines may be set for January. Alternative assessments will only be set for semester 1-only students in lieu of in-person exams in January.

Full year students are expected to sit in-person exams scheduled for both our January and May/June assessment periods -- no special arrangements will be made if you choose to leave London before the programme ends
content.accommodation:
Accommodation for incoming Study Abroad students is available, but not guaranteed

Types of housing and cost

Accommodation Tour (Playlist)

Spaces in King's Residences are limited, so you may need to consider alternative options such as:

Other student housing providers

University of London's Intercollegiate housing or private housing or UoL flatmate finder Facebook group

Sub-letting a room from another King's student. Once you have your KCL email account you can join the King's Flatmate Finder Hub (on MS Teams).

Renting in the private sector

Homestay accommodation is when you stay in the home of a British family. They are particularly suitable for those students wishing to develop their spoken English and those who want to learn more about British culture. There are different homestay options available to choose from: shared or single rooms, bed and breakfast or self-catering, and a range of locations across London. When you make a booking the agent will match you to a suitable family depending on your preferences. For further information, have a look at London Homestays, Britannia Student Services, HFS London, Hosts International, or Perfect Host Homestay.

✏️ Academic Requirements

An Overview of the Academic Programme offered at Durham:

Academic requirements:

Students should have completed at least 1 year of study at the undergraduate level. Students must come for a full academic year (October - June).

English language requirements:

IELTS: 6.5 overall (with no element lower than 6)

TOEFL IBT: overall score of 92 (with no component lower than 23)

*note that all qualifications must be taken within two years of the start of the academic programme.

• Faculty of Law: Students taking courses at the Faculty of Law must have completed 4 semesters at their home university before the begin of their exchange stay at UZH.
• Faculty of Medicine and Veterinary: not open to exchange students.

content.campus_location:
Several courses for exchange students are offered at our Campus Gotland, located in the medieval city of Visby on the island of Gotland in the Baltic Sea. Your students are very welcome to choose Campus Gotland as their campus, please make sure that they are aware of the campus location if they do. Use the filter in the course list to find Campus Gotland courses.

The online application link (not posted on our homepage) opens on 15 March. Deadline for Autumn: 15 April for all students

After creating an account and logging into the application, students will be given more details about the application process.Applicants will need to prepare and upload the following supporting documents:

Official Transcript of Records in English (PDF only)

Course proposal

The updated course list for autumn is available in the Course Catalogue from February. We require extensive information about the students’ course proposal. They will need to show how they meet the entry requirements and have pre-approval from the home university for each choice. In the application, the student must indicate if the course is pre-approved by the home university. If the student chooses, “No approval” or "Pre-approval  will be obtained before arrival", and there are limited places available, we may not be able to secure a place on the course for that student.

What happens after the application has been submitted?

Students will be notified if their application has been successful by mid-June and we kindly ask that they refer to the Fact Sheet for any questions they may have. All other correspondence from us including the Certificate of Acceptance and/or Letter of Admissions with Course Confirmation and more, will be by email only. Please ask students to check their email and spam folders often, since we sometimes need clarifications or other course proposals and our emails have been known to end up in spam-folders. Make sure that they can accept emails from these addresses: mobility@uu.se or noreply@uu.moveon4.com

Accommodation Information

The Housing Application Link and information will be sent directly to the student in May, before the application for housing opens. The link is not published on any website, but details about accommodation alternatives can be found here: www.housingoffice.se.

The application is open 15 – 31 May. Offers will be made by the private company Uppsala University Housing Office at the beginning of June. Please note that an ACTIVE RESPONSE from the applicant is REQUIRED when an offer is made.

Transcript of Records

Please remember that Uppsala University does not automatically send out signed and stamped hard copies of the transcripts of records to the home university. Students are informed that they must download their transcript of records as a verifiable copy after completed exchange studies, using their student account (valid up to six months after the term).

Grading

ECTS grades are not offered to students due to a 2010 decision by the Vice-Chancellor of Uppsala University.Some courses only offer a Fail (U) or Pass (G) grade. Please ask students to check course syllabus for grading scale used if this is an issue for your university.

content.about_text:
All education relies on relevant and current research, and the high quality of SSE’s programs can be maintained because of its collaboration with Swedish and international corporations as well as its dedicated professors and researchers. Further evidence of SSE’s high quality is the School’s EQUIS (European Quality Improvement System) accreditation, which warrants that all degree programs
and research are of top international standard.

SSE works with the international business community as well as highly ranked universities and business schools around the world. Every year SSE welcomes about 250 exchange students, many of whom study within the framework of PIM(Partnership in International Management), a network of
business schools from all over the world. SSE is the only Swedish university in CEMS (The Global
Alliance of Management Education), a community of leading business schools and multinational corporations. SSE is also the only Nordic representative in ICEDR (International Consortium for Executive Development Research).

content.about_text:
Welcome to Madrid! A culture and sports calendar full of events awaits you, as well as a wide range of leisure activities.

Madrid has a magnificent cultural offering: 80 museums, 60 theatres with ongoing stage productions throughout the year, hundreds of restaurants (including 20 with Michelin stars), more than 100 cinemas and more. If you want further information on these venues and more in Madrid, take a look at the following link.

UC3M offers a wide range of information on accommodation, such as halls of residence or residential colleges close to our campuses, shared flats, families, hotels or businesses which have entered into agreements and offer discounts for international students. You can view these via this link. What’s more, there are also international student associations that can help you, such as ESN UC3M which hosts updated information groups via Facebook. You can also make use of the Incoming UC3M exchange Facebook page.

More and more international students are choosing Madrid for an academic stay abroad, and it is already the capital of Spain, one of the cities that rankings like the QS place in the top of destinations where you can better study and live at the same time. There are countless reasons why this magical city is among the favorites of students, but we could highlight, among others, its multiculturalism and cosmopolitan character, its style and quality of life, the opportunity to learn the language, the quality of teaching and the flexibility of its educational system, its historical, cultural and gastronomic heritage, and the fact that it is a generous, welcoming city that never sleeps... You would miss a city like this?

UAB supports and encourages international travel by employees and students for the purpose of furthering the educational, research, and service missions of UAB.  Those traveling on UAB business should be aware that policies and procedures governing behavior and financial affairs apply regardless of the location where UAB business is conducted.  In addition, employees and students are required to adhere to the Protocol for Heightened Review of International Travel to Restricted Locations in order to safeguard the well-being of students, faculty, and staff going abroad.

✏️ Academic Requirements

Students MUST enrol 50% of their credits from their host faculty. The rest can be taken from other faculties (not affiliated schools), but it is not guaranteed.

Students at a master’s level can take master’s courses only if the agreement with their home university allows to do so. Moreover, the master’s coordinator at UAB must ALWAYS give the final approval. To ask for this approval, the student should contact the Exchange Office at the faculty indicating the intended courses.

Language Requirement

As a general rule, it is recommended that students have a minimum language level of B1 on the language of instruction of the courses. However, some faculties have specific language requirements:

Faculty of Economics and Business:

Minimum level of B2 of the language of instruction of the courses selected by the student. List of valid certificates available here:

https://www.uab.cat/web/international-exchange/pathways/uab-incoming-exchange-students/application-form-and-enrolment-process-1345699494272.html

Faculty of Political Science and Sociology

Minimum level of B2 of the language of instruction of the courses selected by the student. Please note that there is a restriction on the number of courses students can attend in English and Spanish, a maximum of 2 subjects in English and 1 subject in Spanish per semester. See more information on the faculty website:

https://www.uab.cat/web/mobilitat-internacional/itineraris/incoming-exchange-students/subject-offer-requirements-1345842938419.html

Faculty of Communication:

Mimium level of B1 of the language of instruction of the courses selected by the student. More  nformation:

https://www.uab.cat/web/international-exchange/itineraris/incoming-exchange-students/specific-information-of-the-faculty-1345783451703.html

Faculty of Psychology:

Minimum level of B1 in Spanish in any case, to enroll courses either in Spanish or in English. Please note that the faculty’s offer of courses in English is 2 subjects per semester and the demand to follow these courses is bigger than the room available, so all students should be able to take courses in Spanish too. See more information on the faculty website:

https://www.uab.cat/web/movilidad-internacional/itinerarios/incoming-exchange-students/subject-list-incoming-students-1345861744077.html

More info about the documents you need to submit to apply for the exchange for UAB

UAB 경영학과 및 스페인어 어학당 수업

UAB 경영학과 수업

경영학과 수업은 가장 영어 수업이 많은 학과 중 하나입니다. 한 학기에 열리는 전체 수업 중 약 절반가량이 영어로 열리게 됩니다.

아래는 24-1에 열린 수업입니다. 위는 스페인어로 진행된 수업, 아래는 영어 수업입니다.

수업 관련 자세한 내용은 UAB 상경대학 홈페이지에서 확인할 수 있습니다. <Study à Bachelor’s degree à subjects and course guides>에 들어가면 경영학과 모든 수업의 수업계획서를 확인할 수 있습니다. <International exchange à Courses, syllabuses and timetables>에 들어가면 해당 학기의 스페인어, 카탈란어, 영어로 진행되는 강의목록과 수업일정을 확인할 수 있습니다. 이외에도 최소이수학점, 학사일정 등 다양한 정보를 확인할 수 있으니, 학기 초 그리고 교환학생을 준비할 때 자주 들어가보고 여러 정보들을 찾아보면 좋을 것 같습니다.

(Faculty of Economics and Business Studies - UAB Barcelona)

교환학생 수강신청은 메일을 통해 공지해줍니다. 24-1 기준, 12월쯤 수강신청을 진행했으며, 현재 7월 중순에 이미 24/25 강의목록이 게재되어 있습니다. 향후 교환학생을 준비하실 경우, 상술된 부분을 감안해 수강신청 일정을 염두해 두시길 바랍니다. 수강신청은 별도의 폼에 자신이 듣고자 하는 강좌를 적는 방식입니다. 선착순의 부담도 없으며, 반드시 수강할 수 있습니다. 관련 절차는 학과 측에서 상세히 공지해주니 반드시 메일을 주기적으로 확인하셔야 합니다.

경영학과 수업은 팀플을 지향해 통계학, 법학 등의 수업에서도 팀플이 진행됩니다. 강의 위주의 수업방식이 아닌 서로 대화를 나누고 의견을 공유하고 토론하듯이 수업이 진행되며, 출석보다는 협업과제와 시험성적이 중요합니다(물론, 출석을 중요시 여기시는 교수님도 계십니다). 전반적으로 한국에 비해 여유로운 분위기 속에서 수업이 진행됩니다.

UAB의 경우 총합 10점 만점에 5점 이상은 패스이며, 5점 이하의 성적을 받았다 하더라도 재시험을 응시할 수 있는 기회가 주어집니다. 10점에는 중간고사, 기말고사, 팀프로젝트 등이 교수님 재량에 따른 비율로 반영됩니다.

24-1을 기준으로, 2월 12일 학기 시작 – 4월 첫째 주 중간고사 - 5월 말 수업 종료 - 6월 한달 기말고사 – 7월 첫째 주 재시험으로 진행되었습니다. 시험 일정도 마찬가지로 상경대학 홈페이지에서 확인할 수 있으며, 재시험 기간은 공식적으로 7월 첫째 주에 진행되는 것이 맞으나, 각 교수님 재량으로 기말 종료 직후에 진행되는 경우도 더러 있었습니다.

UAB 어학당 수업

UAB에서는 학생들을 대상으로 어학수업을 제공해줍니다. 독일어, 영어, 스페인어, 프랑스어, 이탈리아어, 포르투갈어, 러시아어, 카탈란어를 수강할 수 있고, 모두 온라인 수업과 오프라인 수업 모두 가능합니다.

우선 오프라인 수업은 일반 강의와 유사하게 1주일에 2회씩 총 60시간 진행됩니다. 오프라인 수업을 수강하기 위해선 레벨테스트를 봐야 합니다. 일반적으로 개강 2주 내에 시작하기 때문에 그 전에 레벨테스트를 신청해서 응시한 후 수업을 수강하는 것이 바람직합니다. 레벨테스트 역시 매일 정해진 티오가 있는 만큼 미리 신청해야 하며, 수강료는 426유로입니다(교환학생 기준).

오프라인 수업의 경우 일반 인강 강좌와 유사합니다. 수강 레벨은 직접 선택할 수 있습니다. 수강료는 135유로이며, 1년 간 이용 가능합니다.

개인적으로 강의료가 부담되더라도 오프라인 수업을 추천합니다. 온라인 수업의 경우 우리나라의 여타 인강 자료와 차이가 없습니다. 관련 어학 수업은 https://www.uab.cat/languages/ 에 상세히 나와 있습니다.

• Language requirements vary by program (university) and can be found on each program page.  In certain countries, such as Spain, additional proof of language proficiency may be necessary (either a test certificate issued within the past 2 years or an assessment from the home university faculty) in the country's first language, even if the student opts for an English-language program.
• Students must have the required documents prepared when applying. Therefore, students are strongly advised to conduct thorough research on the specific requirements of their preferred universities before submitting their applications. Notably, some UK universities may not accept TOEFL scores, so students applying to these institutions should ensure they possess a valid IELTS score along with the TOEFL score at the time of application. It is important to note that obtaining an IELTS score after the application deadline is not feasible.

content.about_text:
Study abroad through the largest international exchange community!

HOW ISEP EXCHANGE WORKS

Application & Placement Process

An expert ISEP Student Services Officer will help you find programs that fit your academic needs and personal goals.

Yonsei OIA coordinator will assist with your application and help you prepare to go abroad. (abroad@yonsei.ac.kr)

After you submit a complete application, ISEP will place you within your top 3 choices.

You should list up to 5 programs, as Yonsei students are guaranteed admission to their top 3 choice as long as you meet the academic requirements stated in the “Academics” section on the program page. You'll want to pay close attention to the order of host sites on your application; listing a site first means that it's your top choice.

A few weeks after the ISEP Exchange priority deadline, you'll receive your placement. Each student will receive only one placement.

The host institution reviews your application. Once you are approved, you sign your contract and start preparing for your study abroad adventure.

When you arrive abroad, you will be welcomed and supported on-site by your host coordinator and your host university

Program Costs & Fees & Scholarship

You pay your home institution for tuition & fees, housing, and meals.

Tuition: your regular Yonsei tuition fee

Housing: Equivalent to SK Global House Dormitory double room fee (approx 2,000,000 KRW per semester)

Meal: 3 cafeteria meals per day (approx 2,400,000 KRW per semester)

You’ll also want to factor in a few other expenses, including flights, a visa and passport, health insurance, the ISEP application fee, and the placement fee.

Students are required to pay the Application fee and Placement fee

https://www.isepstudyabroad.org/programs/program-types-and-deadlines/isep-exchange

Scholarship Information

https://www.isepstudyabroad.org/planning/finances/study-abroad-scholarships

What’s Included

Meals, housing, and tuition are all included and provided by your host institution

Support from a local onsite host coordinator employed by your host institution, including an on-site orientation

Classes with locals (except for language programs), taught by local faculty

Access to all that your host institution offers, including academic services, libraries, campus facilities, and more

Search for programs within ISEP

visit the program finder: https://search.isepstudyabroad.org/FindAProgram/Map

Type 'Yonsei University' in the pop-up

Select the semester, year, region, and language of instruction. Click 'More filters' and select 'ISEP exchange' under the program section.

**Non-English program applicants must select the language they took their language proficiency test for (비영어권 지원자는 프로그램 언어를 해당 비영어로 선택).

The results will be refined. Upon clicking on a university of interest from the search results, you will be directed to its profile page. Scroll down to the bottom and select its program (it must be ISEP Exchange). Detailed information about the program can be found there. Please be aware that the available areas of study may vary by university.

Students can only apply to exchange programs at universities that have the semester system (Direct program X, quarter system X, trimester system X)

Due to visa issues, students cannot select universities in Italy and France.

Students must check the language requirements for each university. Some countries (i.e. Spain) may require additional proof of language proficiency (either a test certificate issued within 2 years or an assessment of the home university faculty) of the country's main language, even if the student chooses to participate in the English program only.

Students must have the required documents ready when applying, so students are expected to complete in-depth research on their preferred universities' requirements before applying. Some UK universities may not accept TOEFL, so a student trying to apply to these universities needs to have a valid IELTS score along with the TOEFL score when applying. Taking an IELTS score after the placement is not possible.

Prior to your departure, you will pay housing and meal fees to Yonsei University based on the standard set by Yonsei. Once abroad, you will receive housing and meal benefits from your host university, which may be more or less than the exact amount you paid to Yonsei, and the payment methods may vary. Please check the housing and meal plan for each university.

Useful Information and Guidelines for Application

ISEP exchange program Fees and Deadlines

Program Finder

How to Apply

ISEP Placement Process

Application FAQ

https://www.isepstudyabroad.org/connect-with-isep/
